summary: Gruber dismantles Facebook's full-page newspaper ad campaign attacking Apple's iOS privacy changes, specifically the upcoming App Tracking Transparency feature. He notes Facebook deliberately conflates 'free as in freedom' with 'free as in beer' to frame privacy controls as an attack on the free internet. Gruber points out the ad's logical inconsistencies, including conflating websites with apps and mischaracterizing a voluntary software update as 'forced.' He argues the tracking data belongs to users, not Facebook or its advertisers, and compares Facebook's complaint to objecting to blinds being installed on windows it had long peered through. He concludes that Facebook's ad campaign inadvertently makes a stronger case for Apple's privacy initiatives than against them.
key_points: Facebook deliberately exploits the ambiguity of 'free' to frame privacy controls as an economic threat rather than a freedom issue
Apple isn't blocking ads or tracking — it's changing tracking from opt-out to opt-in, giving users awareness and control
The ad conflates websites and apps without explaining the connection, since Apple has no control over web advertising
Facebook's pandemic-era appeal for small businesses is emotional manipulation that implicitly admits they need to invade privacy without user awareness or permission
Apple's counter-response — simply showing the opt-in dialog box — is more effective than Facebook's entire ad campaign
money_quotes: It's an unfortunate quirk of the English language that free as in freedom and free as in beer are very different meanings of free.
What's 'forced' isn't the software update, but Facebook's compliance with new rules that they wish they could ignore.
Facebook's argument is along the lines of arguing that the police shouldn't crack down on burglaries because doing so might hurt pawn shops that have been thriving during a years-long crime spree.
No fair! claims the company objecting to blinds being installed on windows it had long peered through unhindered.
How dare Apple institute improved privacy controls during a severe recession. Boo-fucking-hoo.
If only anyone could remember a time when advertising wasn't based on privacy-invasive tracking, we could know whether there were any successful small businesses back then.
This whole ad reads more like an ad for Apple's privacy initiatives than against them.
key_insight: Facebook's campaign against Apple's tracking transparency inadvertently makes the strongest case for why users need exactly the privacy controls Apple is implementing.
